reasoning: The package is a standard AUR -bin package that downloads a prebuilt .deb from the official PostgreSQL FTP server (ftp.postgresql.org) over HTTPS with a pinned SHA-256 checksum. The PKGBUILD's package() function only extracts data.tar.zst from the .deb into the package directory using standard tar — no network access, no eval, no obfuscation, no .install hooks. Both models reviewed the only meaningful executable file (PKGBUILD) and reached the same conclusion. The .deb binary cannot be statically reviewed by an LLM and is appropriately skipped; its integrity is guaranteed by the pinned checksum against a trusted upstream.
coverage_issues: The .deb binary was skipped by both models (unavoidable for LLM review), so the actual installed payload is not inspected. Integrity rests entirely on the checksum + upstream trust, which is acceptable for a -bin package but means the audit cannot certify the binary contents themselves.
Neither model explicitly confirmed the absence of .install hooks in the package (e.g., post_install) — though the PKGBUILD review implies none exist.
learnings: For -bin packages with only a PKGBUILD + binary artifact, reviewing 1 file (PKGBUILD) is actually complete coverage; the 'shallow (1 file reviewed)' trigger is a false alarm here since the only non-binary file IS the PKGBUILD. The tool should distinguish 'shallow' (skipped reviewable files) from 'minimal package' (no other reviewable files exist).
Both models gave consistent, well-reasoned verdicts focusing correctly on what executes during makepkg/package() — good signal that the prompt guidance on AUR-specific risks is working.

The tool should record whether any .install scripts or hooks are declared in the PKGBUILD as an explicit checklist item, so reviewers can confirm none were silently missed rather than inferring absence.

Consider extracting and listing the .deb's control scripts (preinst/postinst/postrm) as a future enhancement, since those can execute on the user's system at install time even though they are not run during makepkg — this is the only residual unreviewed risk surface for -bin packages.
